data.title: Tencent's Margins, Momentum, And Moat: A Bull Case Building Quietly
data.description:
If you are familiar with my previous articles, you probably know that I am rather bullish about a few Chinese technology companies – including the multinational technology conglomerate Tencent Holdings Limited (OTCPK:TCEHY). In my last article, which was published on February 23, 2025, I argued that a new bull market is taking shape and was rather optimistic about Tencent as an investment.

In the meantime, the stock gained about 3% in value, which sounds not like much but is actually resulting in an annualized return of 13% (not including dividends). We could also say that Tencent is trading almost for the same price as about three months earlier. But in the meantime, the stock declined rather steeply – similar to many other stocks in early April.

On May 14, 2025, Tencent reported first quarter results and aside from looking at the most recent metrics, we will also look at geopolitical developments of the last few months and what implications this might have on Tencent.

Quarterly Results

We start by looking at the first quarter results and revenue increased from RMB 159,501 million in Q1/24 to RMB 180,022 million in Q1/25 – resulting in a growth rate of 12.9% YoY growth for the top line. Operating profit also increased 9.5% year-over-year from RMB 52,556 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 57,566 million this quarter. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased from RMB 4.387 in Q1/24 to RMB 5.129 in Q1/25 – resulting in 16.9% year-over-year bottom line growth.

When looking at the three different business segments, all three contributed to growth. FinTech and Business Services, which struggled a bit in the last few quarters, grew 5.0% year-over-year to RMB 54,907 million in quarterly sales. Marketing Services increased its revenue from RMB 26,506 million in Q1/24 to RMB 31,853 million in Q1/25 resulting in 20.2% year-over-year growth. And finally, Valued Added Services, which is responsible for the biggest part of revenue and grew 17.2% year-over-year to RMB 92,133 million in Q1/25.

Aside from the growing revenue and growing earnings per share, Tencent also improved its operating margin from 36.8% in the same quarter last year to 38.5% in this quarter and gross margin improved from 52.6% in the same quarter last year to 55.8% this quarter. And when looking at the three different segments, each one improved its gross margin – especially FinTech and Business Services improved its margin from 45.6% in the same quarter last year to 50.3% in this quarter.

When looking at further metrics, combined monthly active users of Weixin and WeChat increased from 1,359 million in the same quarter last year to 1,402 million in this quarter – resulting in 3.2% year-over-year growth. The number of fee-based VAS paying users also increased 3.1% year-over-year from 260 million at the end of March 2024 to 268 million at the end of March 2025.

Growth

When looking at revenue growth, we see Tencent accelerating its top line growth in the recent past. In the last five quarters, Tencent constantly reported higher growth rates – from only 6% top line growth (which is still solid) to 13% top line growth in Q1/25.

And I don’t know if Tencent will be able to grow with an even higher pace in the coming quarters. But considering that China is still struggling and GDP growth in China still not back to levels before COVID-19, the revenue growth of Tencent is already high.

One way for Tencent to grow (right now and in the foreseeable future) is by investing heavily in AI. Tencent for example upgraded its advertising platform and is offering now enhanced generative AI capabilities – this is including ad generation, video editing tools or digital human solutions to facilitate live streaming for content creators and merchants.

Tencent is also rolling out several AI features for Weixin – similar to Meta Platforms (META), which is also rolling out Meta AI to be included in WhatsApp. During the last earnings call, management provided updates on this process:

In addition, we're in the early stages rolling out AI features within Weixin. Users can now add Yuanbao as a Weixin context for seamless AI interaction within Weixin Chat, providing context-aware responses and facilitating content discovery while leveraging the Weixin ecosystem and the World Wide Web. Weixin Search is now starting to include results powered by large language models including the fast thinking model HunYuan Turbo S and the Chain of Thought reasoning models HunYuan T1 and DeepSeek R1. We provide AI tools so that content creators can generate images matching the text of their official accounts articles and generate video effects for video accounts videos utilizing preset templates. We reduced Mini Programs development time via an AI coding assistant for creating AI programs that support natural language prompts and image inputs.

Another growth opportunity for Tencent are International Games and revenue in the first quarter of fiscal 2025 grew 23% year-over-year from RMB 13.6 billion in the same quarter last year to RMB 16.6 billion in this quarter. And not only International Games are growing with a high pace – Domestic Games grew 24% year-over-year from RMB 34.5 billion in the same quarter last year to RMB 42.9 billion in this quarter. However, it is worth mentioning that the last quarter was a “low-base quarter” and the high growth rates might be a bit misleading.

I would also assume that the FinTech and Business Services segment might contribute to high growth rates in the years to come. Right now, especially FinTech is rather struggling:

Our total commercial payment volume has been choppy in recent quarters and declined slightly year-on-year in the first quarter but improved in April. To support our wealth management initiatives, we integrated HunYuan and DeepSeek large language model capabilities into our financial assistant chatbot.

While FinTech was rather struggling, business services contributed to solid revenue growth:

Business services revenue returned to a teens year-on-year growth rate in the first quarter, benefiting from higher cloud services revenue as well as increased technology service fees generated from rising e-commerce transaction volumes. Business Services gross margin rose year-on-year to improved efficiencies.

Over the long run, I am optimistic that Tencent will be able to grow its FinTech business with a solid pace. The trend towards digital payments will continue and with the economic situation improving in the coming years, customers might also spend more leading to a higher payment volume.

Aside from growing its top line by investing in AI and other growth strategies, the company might also profit from an improving gross and operating margin in the years to come. I already mentioned above, that both margins improved year-over-year. When looking at the non-IFRS operating margin in the last three years, we see huge improvements. And although margins probably won’t improve with a similar pace as in the last three years, we can assume that margin improvements might contribute 2% or 3% to bottom line growth.

Additionally, share buybacks will also contribute to bottom-line growth. In the last few years, Tencent used share buybacks and although the company is not buying back shares with an aggressive pace, the number of outstanding shares was decreased from 9,704 million in 2021 to 9,261 in the first quarter of fiscal 2025 (the chart below is obviously not updated). When talking about share buybacks, we can also mention that Tencent had RMB 205,253 million in cash and cash equivalents on March 31, 2025, as well as RMB 132,549 million in term deposits on its balance sheet. If Tencent used the cash it has, it could repurchase about 640 million shares (about 7% of shares)

When summing up all the different strategies and contributors to growth, we can assume growth rates in the double-digits in the years to come. Analysts are also quite optimistic for the next few years and assume earnings per share will grow with a CAGR of 11.62% between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2029.

Great Business

I already mentioned above that margins are improving again in the last few years. But when looking at the last ten years, we see that margins were already higher a decade ago. This should also make us optimistic that Tencent might be able to improve its margins further.

The picture is not perfect and Tencent’s margins were certainly fluctuating making it not the best example for stability and consistency. Nevertheless, the improving margins in the last few years are a good sign for the business.

Aside from the margins we are also looking at return on equity as well as the return on invested capital. And RoIC also declined in the last ten years a little bit, but the average RoIC in the last ten years was 17.46% and in the last 5 years, return on invested capital was still 16.73% on average. And as we can see in the following chart, a return on invested capital of 17% is not completely off the charts, but it is certainly a high RoIC and a number many businesses can’t achieve – especially not over the long run.

We can also look at the stock performance over the last 15 years and see an impressive outperformance of Tencent versus the S&P 500.

Of course, the stock performance is always a bit tricky as stocks can over- and underperforman for a long time – meaning that the stock price can be disconnected from the fundamental business performance for 5 or 10 years (or sometimes even longer). But in case of Tencent, I think the stock price performance is another indication of a great business model and a wide economic moat Tencent has around its business.

Tariffs and Uncertainty

Although we can be confident that Tencent is a great business with a wide economic moat, there are certainly risks surrounding the business and the level of uncertainty seems to be quite high.

In general – as argued several times in the past (for example in my last article about Tencent) – I see a huge risk for the United States to be headed for a recession, while China already was in a “recession” in the last few years and might be rather at the beginning of a new bull market.

Nevertheless, we must pay close attention to the trade dispute between the United States and China and what negative impacts tariffs will have on the global economy. A few days ago, it was announced that both countries slashed tariffs. However, the former reciprocal tariffs on Chinese goods of 145% are only suspended for 90 days and there is still a 30% tariff rates on Chinese goods imported to the United States and 10% tariff rate on goods imported to China. And of course, there is reason to be slightly optimistic but expectation stability, consistency or any form of predictability could be a huge mistake. The following chart (although the current tariff rate is probably not accurate anymore) is showing how huge the shift was that was happening in small steps already in the last few years and especially in April 2025.

While I think that the Chinese economy will be able to weather this storm and Tencent in particular is probably one of the companies not affected so heavily by the tariffs, I still think we should be cautious and take into account a scenario where the recovery of the Chinese economy will come to an abrupt end and growth rates for Tencent might decline steeply (or the company might also have to report declining revenue or declining earnings per share). Nevertheless, I remain rather optimistic for Tencent as the business is generating most of its revenue in China. Of course, we should not ignore International Games, which are growing with a high pace (23% in Q1/25) but the segment is responsible for less than 10% of total revenue.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

A final step we must include in every analysis is the calculation of an intrinsic value. Tencent is a great business, but that does not automatically mean the stock must be a great investment right now. Tencent is still trading about 33% below its all-time high and we might conclude that the stock is therefore a bargain. However, Tencent was probably overvalued in early 2021, when it was trading at its all-time high.

A much better way to determine if a stock is over- or undervalued is a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate and the last reported number of shares outstanding, which is 9,261 million. I often take the free cash flow of the last four quarters as basis – in this case RMB 150.5 billion.

In a first rather conservative calculation, let’s assume 10% growth for the next ten years followed by 6% growth till perpetuity. When calculating with these assumptions we get an intrinsic value of RMB 539 (leading to HKD 584) and Tencent would therefore be only slightly undervalued.

However, we can also be a little more optimistic and assume 12% growth for the next ten years and once again 6% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of RMB 621, and HKD 673 and we can call Tencent undervalued.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Tencent is still a “Buy” and the stock still seems to be undervalued and might be trading about 20% below its intrinsic value. Of course, the stock is not a screaming “Buy” and it might not be the best time to purchase the stock anymore. Additionally, the level of uncertainty is extremely high, and we don’t know whether geopolitics might have a huge negative impact on the economy (and Tencent as a business). Nevertheless, I think Tencent is one of the best businesses in the market and I am quite confident that the stock will trade higher in 5 and 10 years from now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JEPQ: A Yield Enhancing Investment With Exposure To Nasdaq-100 Companies
data.description:
Introduction

The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:JEPQ) was established in May 2022. It is an actively managed ETF, which directly invests in U.S. large cap growth stocks, and more specifically, the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) companies.

However, JEPQ is not a regular long-only ETF, which tracks a certain basket of stocks. On top of the long underlying exposure towards the Nasdaq-100 companies, JEPQ employs a covered call strategy. I will elaborate on this later in the article, but this nuance alone fundamentally changes the mechanics of how value and returns are generated.

Speaking of some general facts, there are couple of points that are worth mentioning.

JEPQ's assets under management (AUM) figure stands at ~$22 billion, which makes it one of the largest covered call ETFs out there. The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI) - a related fund, but with exposure towards the S&P 500 companies - has the larger AUM statistic compared to JEPQ, which is logical given the lower volatility (and thus risk) that is associated with the S&P 500.

However, such peers as NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI), REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:FEPI) and Global X Nasdaq-100 Covered Call ETF (NASDAQ:QYLD) have significantly smaller asset sizes.

This also translates to lower fees that JEPQ is able to provide. Even though JEPQ employs such complex processes to generate returns, it has a very low net expense ratio of just 0.35%. For example, QQQI, FEPI and QYLD have net expense ratios of 0.62%, 0.65% and 0.60%, respectively. Over time this kind of difference can compound to something meaningful, imposing a relative drag on the results.

Finally, since JEPQ invests in the Nasdaq-100 related stocks and then sells the index options against them, it means that there is no foreign exchange risk embedded in the ETF.

The Objective of JEPQ

The investment objective of JEPQ is to deliver monthly distributable income and Nasdaq-100 exposure with less volatility. It seeks current income while maintaining prospects for capital appreciation.

The design is calibrated to provide investors with performance that captures a majority of the Nasdaq-100 returns, while reducing the volatility. The design itself is also structured so that under normal circumstances, the monthly income streams remain stable.

In the chart below, we can see how JEPQ has performed since its inception compared to the Nasdaq-100 alternative.

There are a couple of takeaways.

First, about 40% of the total returns that JEPQ has generated over this time period has stemmed from the current income streams. It goes in line with JEPQ's primary strategy to produce attractive distributions.

Second, JEPQ has slightly underperformed the Nasdaq-100, which is logical given the rising markets and the drag that stems from the capped upside through the covered call strategy (more on that later).

Third, the swings in JEPQ's curve are lower, but not materially.

In the following chart we can see a similar pattern that the realized volatility for JEPQ during 2022, when the Fed started to aggressively hike interest rates, was correlated to the Nasdaq-100, but the registered total return loss was lower than for the Index.

This is a great example of how over a sustained period of elevated volatility and a relatively consistent downturn, JEPQ's option-driven defense comes into the play.

Mechanics of value creation

Turning to the process of how JEPQ works, it is important to distinguish the Nasdaq-100 factor, as it automatically introduces greater exposure towards high growth and technology stocks.

However, as briefly explained above, what JEPQ does on top of going long these QQQ stocks is it sells covered call options against them in an attempt to access premium income that could be then distributed to the shareholders.

This is the reason why JEPQ can offer, usually, double-digit dividend yield, while being exposed to the high growth, high-tech companies that tend to distribute small or no dividends.

All of the options are written on an out-of-the-money (OTM) basis, which means that their strike prices are above the prevailing market prices of the underlying portfolio securities (or rather the price level of the Nasdaq-100 index itself).

Having this kind of option overlay approach, completely changes the risk and return structure compared to a regular Nasdaq-100 investment or its underlying securities:

  • The sold call options generate a notable monthly income, where the key driver for how rich the premiums are is the volatility level in the Nasdaq-100 world.
  • The presence of the OTM options limit the upside potential for JEPQ. For example, if the Nasdaq-100 increases in price above the strike value that is associated with the written covered call options, then all these excess returns simply go past JEPQ shareholders. Instead, JEPQ shareholders remain limited to the collected option premiums and the price returns that come from the Nasdaq-100 index hitting the strike values.
  • In case of a downside move in the Nasdaq-100, JEPQ should outperform because of the pocketed option premiums.

Speaking of outperformance and differences from the Nasdaq-100, we have to keep in mind that JEPQ employs an applied data science approach to fundamental research and portfolio construction. In other words, JEPQ does not fully replicate the Nasdaq-100 index when selecting the underlying securities and determining their weights.

Here is a relevant excerpt from the Prospectus:

The adviser utilizes proprietary techniques to process, analyze, and combine a wide variety of data sources, including the adviser’s multi-decade history of proprietary fundamental research, company financial statements, and a variety of other data sources that the adviser finds relevant to conducting fundamental analysis. The adviser combines insights derived from these sources to forecast the financial prospects of each security, also known as fundamental analysis. Alongside its own insights, the Fund’s portfolio management team uses the forecasts developed through data science techniques to help to identify securities that are priced favorably relative to their associated levels of risk.

We can see visual differences when comparing the two tables below.

The Nasdaq-100 Top 10 holdings list:

JEPQ Top 10 holdings list:

While this is another aspect that leads to differences between the Nasdaq-100 and JEPQ returns, it is not as fundamental as the aforementioned option overlay strategy.

Key benefits

From the strategic and long-term investing perspective, JEPQ could benefit investor portfolios in the following ways:

  • Portfolio asset diversification as the Nasdaq-100 driven covered call strategy offers uncommon and unique risk and return exposures.
  • Portfolio income diversification for more dividend oriented investors, who typically source their current income streams from traditional value sectors such as midstream, tobacco, and utilities.
  • Portfolio yield enhancement, where JEPQ's double-digit dividend could boost the overall portfolio weighted average yield.

Key risks for JEPQ

There are two fundamental risks to JEPQ.

First, underperformance stemming from capped upside. As elaborated above, this is a natural consequence of the covered call strategy. For instance, if the Nasdaq-100 surges higher in a similar fashion, as it did in 2023 and 2024, the chances are high that we will see a similar level of negative alpha by JEPQ.

Second, high exposure to the richly priced and high-growth tilted companies in the underlying portfolio base. Even though JEPQ is better protected than a pure-play Nasdaq-100 exposure due to the collected option premiums, the lion's share of its portfolio will still be subject to significantly correlated drawdown as we saw in April 2025 when the tariff announcements rattled the market.

Summary (investor suitability)

In a nutshell, JEPQ is meant for income-oriented investors, who want to diversify their traditional value-tilted holdings with a Nasdaq-100 correlated risk and return factor. There is also a significant value of accessing low double-digit yield that in other alternative segments usually comes with either elevated credit risk or a high external leverage (think of closed end funds).

As elaborated above, JEPQ could be treated as a tactical investment play to benefit from elevated volatility levels and a likely range bound or slightly decreasing Nasdaq-100.

JEPQ is certainly not meant for investors who want to invest for long-term alpha and potentially sizeable returns that could be produced by the high-tech names. The capped upside from written option strike values does not allow JEPQ to fully participate in an upward trending market.

This article answers these three main questions about JEPQ:

  1. What types of investors is JEPQ suitable for?
  2. Does JEPQ's strategy reduce the volatility?
  3. Is JEPQ a good ETF for monthly income?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: LMP Capital And Income Fund Inc. Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • Despite a fast start to the year - a broad-based rally propelled the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) +2.8% in January - the market struggled for traction in the first quarter of 2025. Post-election animal spirits faded in February and March as investors grappled with on again/off again tariff announcements and consternation over the AI outlook. By the end of the quarter, the S&P declined −4.3%, breaking the index's streak of five consecutive quarterly advances.
  • For the quarter, the fund returned −2.71% at NAV, while the benchmark, the 65% S&P 500,35% Bloomberg US Aggregate, returned −1.80%.

QUARTERLY KEY PERFORMANCE DRIVERS

  • Enterprise Products Partners LP (EPD), Plains GP Holdings LP (PAGP) and MPLX shares were higher as an energy-friendly administration took the White House and the trend for more power demand from AI-related data centers continued. Shares also rose as the energy sector outperformed amid rising oil and gas prices.
  • Broadcom (AVGO) was lower as the unveiling of Chinese AI startup DEEPSEEK, whose new foundational and reasoning models - reportedly offering similar performance to leading U.S. models at lower compute costs - triggered a broader selloff in the AI ecosystem.
  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) and Apollo Asset Management (APO) 6.75% convertible preferred shares were down as the market rotated from alternative asset managers, which performed well in 2024.

Outlook & Strategy

  • Market risks remain elevated as a result of anemic economic activity due to still-high interest rates, tight financial conditions and heightened political uncertainty, particularly in the areas of tariffs, taxes and fiscal spending priorities. The cloud of unpredictability and uncertainty surrounding these topics is causing elevated volatility on an almost daily basis. The war in the Middle East is nearing its final stages and the risk to the overall market appears to be contained. The U.S. housing market remains weak, in large part due to still-high mortgage rates and weakening economic fundamentals. Corporate earnings are solid but remain under pressure from a slowing economy and high interest rates, which puts upward pressure on interest expense. We believe the Federal Reserve will pause its rate decreases for the time being, but we continue to anticipate additional cuts in 2025, which we view as a positive for the economy more broadly and risk assets such as stocks in particular.
  • As always, we carefully weigh short-term risks against the longer-term backdrop, and our assessment is that the environment remains challenging for risk assets such as common stocks in the short term. In the long term, we continue to be comfortable owning high-quality companies that exhibit leadership positions in their sectors, have sound balance sheets, generate strong free cash flow and offer attractive dividend yields and dividend growth potential. In particular, we seek out and analyze those companies that can represent the next generation of dividend aristocrats and have identified a number of our existing holdings that could reach that status over time.
  • In recognizing the current volatile investment landscape, we have been increasing our positions in defensive names as evidenced by our recent initiations in Walmart (WMT) and PepsiCo (PEP) in consumer staples and McKesson (MCK) in health care. Indeed, we have increased our focus on more defensive sectors like consumer staples and health care. Those sectors are also very good hunting grounds to find high-quality (emerging) dividend aristocrats, which typically offer solid dividend profiles while providing more stability during turbulent times. By the same token, we have diminished our positions in more cyclical sectors such as information technology and alternative asset managers and used the proceeds as a source of funds for these more defensive companies. We continue to carefully assess the risks and rewards in our exposures and recognize that the investment environment has become increasingly volatile.
  • We remain opportunistic and tactical in managing through the high level of volatility in the energy market. Oil supply remains adequate for the time being and the risk of escalation of the war in the Middle East involving oil-related infrastructure such as those in Iran appears diminished. We continue to emphasize balance sheet strength, asset footprint diversity and quality, and we carefully assess our holdings with an eye toward managing successfully through the short-term disruption and thriving in the long term. We continue to believe that in the long term, midstream companies and MLPs continue to represent an attractive investment opportunity as the U.S. continues to cement its status as an energy superpower and exhibit sustained hydrocarbon production increases, which can bode well for high-quality energy MLPs as volumes to be processed are poised to increase over time.
  • With relative weakness in the REIT sector over the past year, largely a result of high interest rates, we continue to assess whether our exposure warrants a change. For the time being, we are content holding onto to our positions. As always, we emphasize those REITs with strong balance sheets, ample cash flows, leadership positions in their sectors and attractive valuations. In particular, we continue to hold infrastructure REITs such as data center REITs and telecommunication REITs where growth opportunities remain robust due to the buildout of artificial intelligence and cloud computing. We believe these next-generation REITs are well-positioned to capitalize on this large opportunity ahead.
  • We continue to believe alternative asset managers are an attractive long-term opportunity as many institutional investors seek uncorrelated returns, which these alternative asset managers can provide. Further, they operate in a loosely regulated environment, unlike their large bank counterparties, providing a competitive and sustainable advantage. We particularly like the sector leaders and we expect the growth of assets under management for them to continue to increase.
  • We remain bullish about the growth prospects for our technology holdings, especially those holdings that have the prospect for outsize growth opportunities in the artificial intelligence and cloud computing ecosystem. This includes hyperscalers, select software companies, semiconductor investments and some energy and utility companies. In our view, the portfolio is well-positioned to capitalize on these trends, which we believe to be large and long term in nature.

data.title: Alliance Entertainment: A Microcap Turnaround The Market Is Missing
data.description:
Thesis

Alliance Entertainment Holding Corporation (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:AENT) is one of those little companies that most people have never heard of. Officially, it’s a microcap outfit in the world of physical media and collectibles distribution. But despite its modest size, it has carved out an interesting niche for itself, acting as a middleman between some of the world’s biggest entertainment brands and a surprisingly persistent population of consumers who still prefer their music, movies, and games in forms they can actually hold. Its product lineup is broad, everything from DVDs and vinyl records to action figures and video games, and its client base reads like a roll call of modern retail, with names like Walmart (WMT), Amazon (AMZN), and Target (TGT). In fact, for a business of its scale, Alliance is impressively well put together, with infrastructure, licensing deals, and direct-to-consumer logistics that you might expect from a firm several times its size.

Against this backdrop, the company’s Q3 earnings for fiscal 2025, released just yesterday, suggest signs of progress, increased automation, tighter margins, and some success with exclusive licensing deals, all of which point to a business that is, at the very least, demonstrating the contours of a turnaround.

Earnings Momentum

In the muted language of quarterly earnings conference calls, companies often try to boast triumphs without drawing too much attention to the prior wreckage. But Alliance’s third fiscal quarter of 2025 doesn’t so much boast as uncoil a tightly wound spring of operational discipline finally releasing tension. While the numbers are, on their face, just numbers - $4.9 million in adjusted EBITDA, a 66% spike from the previous year’s $2.95 million - I think what’s happening underneath them is less about celebration and more about restitution. Over the trailing twelve months, adjusted EBITDA crept up to $26.4 million, and even the margin ticked up from 2.2% to 2.5%.

The $1.9 million in net income this quarter, a sharp reversal from the $3.4 million loss one year ago, is the manifestation of an effort to claw back control over basic cost mechanics. You don’t swing net income by $5.3 million without yanking on a lot of levers. And when nine-month net income goes from $2.1 million to $9.3 million, an almost 349% increase, you’re looking at hard choices paying off.

Underneath the earnings, Alliance was waging a small war against its own logistics sprawl. Distribution and fulfillment costs drifted down 10.2% year-over-year, driven by the closure of a 162,000-square-foot warehouse in Minnesota. Enter automation. With robotic systems like AutoStore and Sure Sort X now humming along, the company claims over $900,000 in annualized savings. These are cost cuts designed to elevate gross margin, which, to that point, edged up from 13.2% to 13.6%.

At the same time, Alliance turned its attention to tidying up the balance sheet. Inventory was brought down to $93.2 million from $108 million the year before while accounts payable crept up from $132.5 million to $139.6 million. The effect of all this was to give the company some more breathing room in the cash flow department. More importantly, it helped them match what they had on hand with what customers actually wanted.

Expanding Channel Reach

With a 7-point jump in direct-to-consumer revenue, from 33% to 40% of gross in a single year, the company is stitching together a channel strategy that doesn’t rely on any one storefront, instead spreading risk and reach across a widening mix of partners: legacy giants like Walmart and Amazon, alongside volatile but potent newcomers like Temu, Shein, and Instagram. It’s a kind of hedged bet on retail’s fragmented future.

And yet, on April 24, 2025, Alliance walked away from a $72.2 million acquisition of Diamond Comic Distributors, a deal it had already fought for and won in bankruptcy court. After outmaneuvering competitors, weathering legal headwinds, and having its winning bid reinstated, Alliance still bailed. The very next day, director Thomas Finke resigned. Whether that’s connected is anyone’s guess, but the optics aren’t exactly subtle.

It’s tempting to read that exit as an act of corporate cold feet. But when viewed in the context of Alliance’s balance sheet-tightening, its warehouse closures, and its integration of automation systems designed to strip out labor costs and squeeze margin out of every square foot, it looks more like consistency. Diamond’s portfolio: comic books, toys, games, and tangled international logistics was, if nothing else, a walking contradiction to the lean, brand-focused, margin-disciplined operation Alliance is trying to become. I think buying it would have been a regression: a lateral move into complexity, capital expenditure, and integration risk that could have undone everything the company spent the last year stabilizing.

Compare that to Alliance’s earlier pickup of Handmade by Robots in December 2024. Here was a company purpose-built for the new Alliance playbook: modest scale, defensible IP, pop culture relevance, and distribution-ready products with digital-native branding. And through integration with BDA, Alliance bolted it into a vertically aligned go-to-market machine.

Which brings me to the broader point: Alliance is betting on leverage. That’s clearest in its licensing portfolio, which pulled in $250 million in revenue over the past year, roughly one-quarter of the company’s total sales. The Paramount agreement, inked in January 2025, handed Alliance exclusive U.S. and Canadian rights for physical media, including DVDs, Blu-rays, and specialty collector formats like SteelBooks. Early returns, anchored by tentpole titles like Gladiator 2, Sonic 3, and Yellowstone, have already proven accretive. I get it. It all reads like ancient tech, but the takeaway isn’t that physical media is making a comeback, it’s that, even in a supposedly declining format, exclusivity and fandom still print money if you own the channel.

Valuation, Growth Potential, and Market Dislocation

When a company’s forward price-to-earnings ratio gets sliced to 7.17x while analysts are forecasting a 162.2% earnings-per-share surge by June 2025, something isn’t syncing up. Markets are usually pretty good at sniffing out momentum, so when that kind of EPS growth gets met with a P/E that’s less than half its trailing multiple of 18.79x, what you’re looking at is doubt, the kind that suggests investors see a trapdoor under the headline numbers.

That skepticism, however, is swimming upstream. Because internally, AENT, after coughing up red ink, $35.4 million in net losses in 2023, has flipped the script. In the TTM, it posted $6.6 million in net income. It’s an earnings U-turn, driven by better inventory discipline, tighter cost structures, and more streamlined working capital

And then there’s the cash. In 2022, AENT was hemorrhaging $83 million in operating cash flow. A few years later, it’s bringing in $43.3 million. That’s down from a $55.8 million peak, sure, but still a staggering improvement from where it started.

Levered free cash flow sits at $34.7 million, roughly $0.70 per share, and capital expenditures are negligible at $7.6 million.

To some extent, the market’s reluctance can be explained by the space AENT inhabits. Distribution doesn't scale fast. And it doesn’t usually command premium multiples. But even by those standards, the valuation compression is extreme.

The company currently trades at a blended P/E of 13.24x, well below its own historical average of 33.28x and under its estimated fair value multiple of 37.40x. Overall, there’s a strange kind of financial schizophrenia playing out at AENT. On the one hand, you have a company executing a dramatic internal cleanup, posting real improvements in cash flow, earnings, and capital discipline. But on the other, you’ve got a business that can’t seem to get out of its own way on the revenue side, dragging down its valuation and raising uncomfortable questions about whether structural efficiency alone can offset shrinking top-line relevance.

Start with the debt. AENT is sitting on $105.18 million in total debt, stacked against a market cap of just $131.47 million. In practical terms, that means debt comprises nearly half of the company’s $234.16 million enterprise value. That kind of leverage would normally be a red flag. But here’s where it gets strange: in fiscal 2024, AENT repaid $1.05 billion in debt. That’s not a typo.

Just look at the revenues this microcap is bringing in. It’s a massive number for a company this size and signals something uncharacteristically aggressive, namely, that management is going after deleveraging with a kind of urgency.

And yet, the market’s not biting. That because, underneath all that financial tightening, the revenue data is wobbling. For the quarter ending March 31, 2025, revenue dropped 3.2% year-over-year, slipping from $863.5 million to $835.7 million. Gross profit followed suit, falling to $96.9 million from $102 million suggesting that, despite the company’s operational clean-up, AENT is struggling to actually grow. And in a low-margin business, that’s a problem. If your gross profit is sliding while your revenue shrinks, any hope of EPS growth is tethered almost entirely to squeezing more blood from the same operational stone.

That tension is particularly acute in the gaming division, a segment that once carried growth potential but now looks like dead weight. The company blamed limited hardware allocations from Microsoft during fiscal 2025, essentially saying there wasn’t enough product to sell. This stands in stark contrast to the prior year, when promotional pushes helped juice demand. Worse, the delayed release of Grand Theft Auto VI to May 2026 means there’s no near-term software catalyst, either. So while there’s speculation that the rumored Nintendo Switch 2 might eventually revive interest, for now, visibility in this segment is close to nonexistent.

Put together, what you get is a company with a radically improving financial core trapped inside a revenue model that isn’t moving fast enough to justify optimism. That helps explain why the market’s treating AENT more like a question mark.

Headwinds

On paper, it’s easy to go all in on the narrative for a comeback: improved cash flow, tightened operations, an aggressive deleveraging campaign that saw over $1 billion in debt retired in fiscal 2024. But zoom in a little closer to make sure you understand the risks tied to this microcap, and the contradictions begin to stack up. For starters, that margin profile is already razor-thin. A trailing twelve-month EBITDA margin of that 2.5% I mentioned earlier leaves very little slack for surprises, and yet, surprises keep coming.

Take tariffs, for instance. The Handmade by Robots product line, one of the company’s few clear growth levers, was temporarily knocked off course by a tariff spike that reached 145%. Even now, with rates cooled to 30%, the aftershocks haven’t disappeared. Planning cycles have been disrupted, cost assumptions are out of sync, and unless that volatility smooths out, margin forecasts will likely need a haircut. The arcade product category faces similar pressure: import costs are pushing retail prices higher.

Then there’s the company’s pivot toward DTC. In execution, Alliance now leans heavily on giants like Walmart, Amazon, Target, and Best Buy to move its DTC volume. That’s a dependency. And in a softening macro environment, all it would take is one cost renegotiation or strategic shuffle by these partners to compress fulfillment margins and slam the brakes on throughput.

With management aiming, optimistically, for a 5% EBITDA margin long-term, and hoping just to edge past 3% in fiscal 2026, this seems more like a pressure point. On one side, you have impressive financial discipline: a company that went from torching $83 million in operating cash in 2022 to generating $43.3 million just a few years later. On the other, you've got a business model that still hinges on volume gains and price stability in segments where neither are guaranteed. The collectibles unit can’t absorb tariff shocks forever. The arcade division might stall under price fatigue. And the DTC channel, for all its upside, is tethered to retail middlemen with far more leverage.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’d call AENT, a high-risk microcap, a “Buy” at this point. The company is clearly getting its financial act together, margins are widening, cash flow is good, and its strategic direction actually seems to make sense. Right now, the market still doesn’t seem to believe it. Yes, revenue growth is somewhat limp and there are still risks around execution, but the current price doesn’t really reflect the progress they’ve made or the potential for more.

data.title: MediWound Ltd: Undervalued High Growth Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am bullish on MediWound Ltd (NASDAQ:MDWD) and rate it a buy. The company is offering investors a rare blend of revenue growth and high potential pipeline. If balance sheet strength is your consideration, this stock fits your criteria as well. The compelling story about this investment opportunity is wrapped up by its undervaluation, with a massive upside potential.

Company Overview

MDWD is a manufacturer and distributor of novel bio-therapeutic solutions for tissue repair and regeneration. Its main focus is enzymatic debridement for burns and chronic wounds. NexoBrid, its flagship product is both FDA and EMA approved as a biologic for eschar removal in both deep partial and full thickness thermal burns. In its pipeline is a promising product, EscharEx, which is in its phase 3 clinical trials.

NexoBrid Commercial Success Driving Solid Revenue Growth

The company’s lead product is demonstrating a notable commercial trajectory. For example, in 2024, NexoBrid generated $20.2 million in sales, up from $18.69 million the previous year. In addition, it is estimated that this year, revenue will hit about $24 million, marking a 19% YoY growth. The main growth driver here is expanding adoption in the US, Japan and EU specifically for pediatric burn care. This follows a publication of phase 3 CIDS data in February 2025, which confirmed NexoBrid’s superiority over other products.

I think this company is set to experience stellar upward momentum in sales because besides its commercial success, its orphan drug status offers market exclusivity, shielding it from generic competition up to 2030.

When I talk about commercial success, I am referring to the 42% growth in hospital orders in Q4 2024. With the current demand exceeding supply capacity, the company is scaling up production with a new GMP-compliant facility. By the end of this year, output is expected to increase sixfold, something I believe will sustain strong revenue growth in the future.

EscharEx, A Promising Product In Phase III

MDWD’s next product is a potential game changer in the chronic wounds market. It is developed for enzymatic debridement of chronic wounds such as the VLUs and diabetic foot ulcers. I have several positives from this product’s milestones. To begin with, it has shown superior efficacy when compared to collagenase in Phase 2 trials. This positions the product as a potential market leader in non-surgical debridement in VLUs.

Further, the value phase 3 was initiated in 2024, an important trial for the product. Results are expected by 2026. I am confident that this product is likely to succeed based on previous data both in efficacy and safety. For instance, 78% of EscharEx patients achieved wound bed preparation, versus 38% of SANTYL, with a 33% wound closure rates compared to SANTYL’s 25%. Given these results, EscharEx has a strong foundation for phase 3 trial success.

If it succeeds, it will address a massive unmet chronic wound market. Take an example of the US alone, where more than 6.5 million patients incur a cost of about $25 billion in treating chronic diseases.

With the rising incidence of diabetes and demand for non-surgical treatment, this product has a high market potential. I find the product’s alignment with the new LCD policy, which requires complement wound debridement before applying tissue substitute, very crucial. This is because it strengthens its market and commercial viability.

In my view, owing to its high prevalence of chronic wounds and the limitation of existing products, this product's approval could drive exponential revenue growth positioning it as a cornerstone for MDWD's long term value proposition.

Strong Balance Sheet

Most investors tend to be bothered by weak balance sheets for growing companies. Interestingly, this company’s balance sheet is very robust. With a total debt of $6.9 million and a cash balance of $43.2 million, its net cash stands at $36.3 million. This not only reflects on its deleveraged status but also its strong liquidity. Notably, its total equity of about $31.2 translates to a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.22 which is an indication of low debt risk.

With technically zero debt(negative net debt), this company’s financial standing is solid. This offers ample runway to fund future developments as well as overcome potential economic headwinds. For example, its net cash is adequate to fund its 2024 R&D expense more than 4x. This implies that the company has approximately a 4-year runway in its R&D expenses. The solid balance sheet reduces the company’s financial risks thus appealing to risk-averse investors.

Market Trends Tailwinds

As the aging population grows, there are rising cases of diabetes and increasing demand for non-surgical treatment options.

These trends have resulted in a rapid growth in the wound care market. It is estimated to continue growing consistently to $28.6 billion in 2028, up from $21.5 billion in 2023.

The company’s product alignment with industry trends such as the shift toward non-surgical interventions and the new LCD policy enhances its competitive market position. As a result, I expect the company to capitalize on these trends for its long term growth path.

Valuation

I think this company’s market cap of about $201 million is low compared to its potential. Given the significant addressable market for its products, I believe that if EscharEx is approved, annual sales would exceed $200 million. This estimate assumes that the company captures about 0.69% of the projected global wound care market size by 2028. Applying a PS of 5x, of course a conservative one given the forward PS of 8.41x, MDWD market cap would be about $1 billion post approval of EscharEx. This is a significant upside potential. Given the promising results in the previous trial stage, I believe it’s very likely this product will be approved.

Q1 2025: Expectations And Areas Of Focus

MediWound is expected to release its Q1 2025 results on May 21. One of the Key metrics to watch is revenue growth. I expect revenue to be in the region of $6 million beating the estimated $5.2 million, driven by its NexoBrid’s accelerating adoption (42% hospital adoption growth in Q4 2024). I am anticipating EPS to be at about -$0.55, a reflection of its ongoing R&D and commercialization efforts.

Alongside financial metrics, investors should pay attention to any updates on its two major products. For the NexoBrid, look for insights about accelerated production expansion, while for EscharEx, look for any updates on phase 3 developments.

Generally, the earnings call is likely to highlight clinical progress, commercialization strategies and financial sustainability. Any positive updates on these key areas could catalyze the stock upwards in the short run and vice versa. As such, I expect some volatility in the coming weeks.

Risks

Although I am optimistic about this stock, several risks warrant consideration:

  • Clinical trial risk: The viability of its product pipeline depends on the outcome of its phase 3 results. Any delays or negative results could impact the stock and delay its financial growth.
  • Regulatory hurdles: Should there be delays in EscharEx FDA and EMA approvals, the company’s growth trajectory would be halted hurting he market sentiments.
  • Economic headwinds: Given the 2.7% economic contraction in the US in Q1 2025 and the rising concerns about healthcare expenditure, MDWD's cash reserve is at risk. Although it mitigates the risk in the short term, a prolonged downturn might affect investor confidence.

Conclusion

I recommend this stock to long term oriented investors targeting a return of about 5x by 2028. This shall be driven by EscharEx’s commercialization and NexoBrid’s expansion. Keep a close eye on phase 3 trial progress for effective risk management.

data.title: Harbour Energy: A Double Digit Dividend Yield From European Natural Gas
data.description:
Introduction

As I have been increasing my position in Harbour Energy (OTCPK:PMOIF) in the past few months, I’m planning to keep close tabs on the company’s performance given the current volatility on the oil and gas markets. I mainly like Harbour’s exposure to European natural gas prices which currently trade at approximately 35 EUR per MWh (which is more than three times higher than the current Henry Hub natural gas price).

Harbour has its primary listing is on the London Stock Exchange where the company is trading with HBR as its ticker symbol. With an average daily volume of in excess of 4 million shares, the London listing for sure is the best listing to trade in the company’s stock. The company’s website contains quite a few ‘download only’ links, but you can find all relevant information I’ll be referring to here.

2025 will be the first full financial year after the large acquisition

While the company doesn’t publish detailed financial results on a six month basis, it does provide quarterly trading updates which allows the market to piece things together. In this article I’ll first discuss the Q1 trading update before moving on to the implications Although those are not representative of the new, larger Harbour Energy, it does provide a few useful elements.

In the first quarter of the current financial year, Harbour Energy produced approximately 500,000 barrels of oil-equivalent per day with approximately 60% of the oil-equivalent output consisting of natural gas. 40% of the output was classified as liquids while another 40% was natural gas sold at the [high] European prices with the remaining 20% of the production mix consisting of non-European natural gas. The country with the largest contribution to the production mix is now Norway, which represents in excess of a third of the oil-equivalent production.

The unit costs continued to decrease thanks to the strong operational performance and the low-cost (and gassy) nature of the assets that were owned by Wintershall DEA which was acquired by Harbour. The production cost decreased from $18/boe to just $13/boe, resulting in healthy margins.

It’s perhaps also worth emphasizing the company is still actively managing its hedge book and for 2025, it has hedged about 18 million barrels of oil at $75/barrel while the natural gas hedges were priced at $12.5/mscf. This year’s hedges cover approximately 40% of the anticipated natural gas and liquids production.

And even for next year, the hedges look fine with decent oil hedges at US$73/barrel although the natural gas price will drop to $10.5/mscf on a hedged basis but it goes without saying that the higher market prices will compensate for the lower hedging price, and Harbour Energy remains exposed to the strong European natural gas market.

The strong performance in the first quarter also allowed the company to tighten a few elements of its full-year guidance. The average production rate will now come in at 455,000-475,000 boe/day (the lower end of the guidance was increased by about 5,000 boe/day) while the total capex was reduced as well to $2.4-2.5B (coming from $2.4-2.6B as shown below). The total capex will trend down in the next few years as from 2026 on the average annual capex will decrease to $1.7-2B per year, and this step down could boost the net free cash flow by 30-40% per year.

The lower oil and natural gas prices had a negative impact on the anticipated free cash flow result, and the company now expects to generate $900M in net free cash flow, based on the midpoint of the production guidance and the capex guidance. This is based on an average Brent oil price of just $68/barrel and considering Brent was $75 in Q1, this basically implies a Q2-4 2025 Brent oil price of around $65/barrel. That’s also much higher than the company’s internal threshold to commit to the construction of a project. The company targets IRR’s above 20% while the breakeven point for new project has to be achieved at an oil price below $40/barrel and a natural gas price below $5/mscf.

And as the image above shows, Harbour Energy will still have to pay billions in taxes. The total tax bill is now estimated at in excess of three times the net free cash flow and it is no secret Harbour Energy is still actively looking to exit its UK operations as the tax structure is just very prohibitive.

Most of the larger investments are indeed occurring outside of the UK. The company has taken a final investment decision on the 15% owned Southern Energy LNG project in Argentina where its local natural gas will be liquefied. Additional efforts to increase production in Mexico and Indonesia are underway.

Meanwhile, Harbour Energy remains protective of its balance sheet. The company has an investment grade rating from both Moody’s and Fitch as the net debt decreased to $4.2B. It should be possible to reach a net debt position of $4B by the end of this year (the net free cash flow in the next few quarters will be relatively low due to the timing of investments and tax payments. With an EBITDAX (EBITDA before exploration) of $4B in 2024 (and this will increase sharply this year considering the acquisition contributed just a few months to the FY 2024 EBITDAX result), the debt level appears more than acceptable.

Takeaways

I have been increasing my long position in Harbour Energy in the past few weeks and months as it is one of the easier ways to gain exposure to the strong European natural gas prices. The dividend policy is quite generous: at the current share price the implied dividend yield is 11% (using the company’s policy to pay out $455M per year in dividends) at a payout ratio of approximately 50%.

I hope the company can exit its UK operations and redeploy capital in areas and regions where the fiscal regime isn’t as prohibitive.

data.title: AMD: Inference Is Hitting An Inflection Point
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig.

Investment Thesis

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) shares are up almost 5% since I last wrote on the company in February despite an overall sliding stock market. However, shares are down nearly 4.8% year-to-date, and they’re down more than 23.14% in the last year. While price action has gone in the wrong direction overall over the last 12 months, I remain bullish on AMD, and I still think shares have strong upside potential on the back of inference demand starting to shine. I think the market has started to notice this since February. Given the company’s improving fundamentals, I view this year's long pullback as a buying opportunity. Recent trends since earnings have only solidified what, I think, is the company’s strong foundation.

I see AI inference emerging as a major growth driver. I believe that hyperscalers will spend an estimated $371 billion on data-center compute in 2025 (up 44% year-over-year) and by 2032, nearly half of AI spend will shift to inference. In essence, AI inference spending is a growing market that AMD is uniquely positioned to take advantage of.

While their growth is slower than the triple digit growth the market saw from their competitor Nvidia (NVDA) last year, I believe AMD has some of the best data center momentum of any tech company out there. In Q1, AMD’s data center revenue surged 57% YoY to $3.7 billion. This growth was driven by CPU share gains and “strong growth of AMD Instinct GPUs” for AI workloads.

With their frankly unique ecosystem of leading CPUs, GPUs and adaptive chips, I think AMD could more than double its AI data-center revenue in the coming years. Wall Street forecasts ~20% annual revenue growth through 2026 (to about $37.4 billion) and overall ~60% EPS growth in 2026 reflecting this optimism. I actually think these assumptions are conservative.

The era of inference AI computing is here. With this, I remain a strong buy on shares of AMD.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

While earnings were last week, I want to point out that this is not just a Q1 recap. Although AMD did report what, I thought, were strong Q1 figures the week ago Tuesday, this article goes beyond rehashing quarter-end figures. I want to focus on how Q1 confirmed what, I think, is an AI inflection. I think the devil is in the details here.

After AMD posted a slight data-center miss in Q4 2024, the company delivered $7.44 billion in revenue (+36% YoY) and $0.96 EPS in Q1, beating estimates and prompting management to highlight “very strong adoption” of new AI products. Wall Street has been worried that Nvidia is doing too well and leaving little space for AMD in the AI GPU market. I believe this cannot be further from the truth.

I think the ramp of Instinct MI325X shipments and commentary on double-digit AI revenue growth are clear indicators that inference demand is hitting a turning point. I believe AMD is the clear leader in this section of the market. Shares deserve a higher forward P/E multiple. This is why I am doing follow-up coverage.

Inference At An Inflection Point

One of the things that excites me most about AMD is that inference demand appears to be surging. I think we are reaching a real turning point.

During a week ago Tuesday’s earnings call, AMD management noted that the company experienced “significant double-digit” AI revenue growth as MI325X GPUs ramped across 35+ cloud platforms.

Turning to our data center AI business, revenue increased by a significant double-digit percentage year over year, as MI325X shipments ramp to support new enterprise and cloud deployments. More than 35 MI300-series platforms are in production from all the leading service providers, supporting the expanding number of Instinct GPU deployments with cloud, enterprise, and AI customers.

Recent breakthrough models like OpenAI’s “o3” and even DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) R1 require far more compute per query, shifting spend from training to inference. I believe it’s clear that AMD is capturing far more of demand than they did during the pre-training part of the AI cycle.

I think AMD’s MI300-series is a huge player right now. MI300X’s 192GB HBM advantage (compared to Nvidia’s 80GB) enables single-GPU inference of 66B-parameter LLMs and attracts Meta and Microsoft for large-scale deployments.

Microsoft and Meta are not new customers. What matters is that inference compute is growing fast enough where they should grab wallet share at these key hyperscalers.

So I think overall what we're seeing is with these new reasoning models, the inferencing is more important. And there is also a move to more distributed inferencing. So, I think that plays into our strengths. I think, we have demonstrated that with MI300 that we are an excellent inference solution. And that holds true for 355 and 350 series as well.

In my opinion, AMD’s shift to bi-weekly ROCm releases, day-zero support for LLaMA 4, GEMMA 3, and R1, and over 2 million Hugging Face models now running on AMD hardware means their ecosystem is spreading exponentially.

Really, this is the inflection point for inference.

I think AMD’s memory and interconnect advantages with their complete CPU to GPU ecosystem position the company in a great position. I think this call confirmed what the market is already showing: inference has reached its inflection point.

Adding to this, we’re starting to see key AI startups like TensorWave power their strong growth primarily on AMD architecture. TensorWave this week announced a $100 million Series A financing round.

The GPUs they use? 8,000 of AMD’s MI325X series.

I think this funding round shows how a key part of the future of inference will be done on AMD GPU’s.

Valuation

AMD has a very strong growth outlook. Consensus estimates see FY2025 revenue of $31.62 billion (+22.4%) and FY2026 revenue of $37.29 billion (+17.94%), with EPS rising from $4.01 to $5.95. In my opinion, I actually think these figures are conservative if inference continues to accelerate.

Right now, AMD shares sit around $117 per share (even despite the impressive buyback program announced this week). I believe there is room for multiple expansion across the board with most valuation metrics. Looking at AMD’s forward PEG ratio, their current forward valuation comes in at 1.10, which is almost 36% below the sector median of 1.72. This is also 13.79% below the company’s five-year average of 1.28. Seeking Alpha Quant gives AMD a grade of an A- on this metric.

On this same front, the company has a forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 28.02. Although this is actually currently higher than the sector median of 22.33, this is still a 31.32% difference to the company’s five-year average of 40.81. AMD earns a grade of a C on this metric.

AMD’s Q1 gross margin hit 54% (+140 bps YoY) in Q1. I really think the GPU maker is starting to fire on all cylinders. The market should reward them for this.

With this, I believe that shares should trade at the sector median forward PEG ratio. If we saw this happen, this would imply 56.36% upside in shares.

Risks

While I've been bullish on AMD, no company comes without at least a few risks. One of my bigger concerns is that the company has to execute near flawlessly on their software/ecosystem execution. AMD has to continue to narrow the CUDA gap because while CUDA is less valuable in an inference environment (vs. a pre-training cluster), any lag coming from their ROCm driver quality or their framework support will likely mean slow adoption despite what, I think, are clear hardware advantages for inference and TCO (total cost of ownership). AMD has a clear path to show they are the cheaper option in the inference space. But in an industry that is still flooded with funding, cost is not everything.

The other big risk I am concerned about are potential export controls on their GPUs to China. According to Reuters, U.S. curbs forced a $1.5 billion revenue hit in 2025. I think if the trade war continues to wreak havoc between two of the world’s biggest economies, we're going to see AMDs top-line growth come in slower than expected.

The silver lining here is that the Trump administration is looking to go the other way. Just in the last three days, the administration has explored rewriting the Biden era chip restrictions. AMD stock has rallied on this. I think these overhauls destroy one of the biggest bear theses.

Bottom Line

While this research is not just about Q1, if we learned anything from the quarter, it’s that AI inference is finally at a pivotal moment for AMD. Not only is it pivotal, it's here now.

If AMD can dominate in this sub AI GPU field, I believe they will be incredibly successful against competitors like Nvidia.

With this, I think AMD has an attractive risk/reward ratio. Less than one year ago, shares were worth over $180. Since then, they’ve dipped all the way down to $115/share range. I continue to believe AMD offers AI upside at an extreme discount (compared to peers), and their continued execution (led by CEO Lisa Su) should drive a forward PEG multiple expansion as the inference story unfolds. I think it would be a huge-missed opportunity to miss out on a company with strong high AI upside like AMD at such a large discount. Really, I think the last few weeks have pushed the market to think differently about AMD. Shareholders should be rewarded.

With this, I am sticking with my strong buy stance from February.
